jsonLdWeb.name: Post Hole Concrete Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate bags of concrete needed for fence posts, deck posts, and mailbox posts.

mainEntity.name: How many bags of concrete for a fence post?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 4×4 post in a 10" diameter hole at 24" deep needs about 1.5 bags of 80 lb concrete. At 36" deep, plan for 2-3 bags per post. The exact amount depends on hole diameter, depth, and post size.

mainEntity.name: How deep should a fence post hole be?

acceptedAnswer.text: The standard rule is 1/3 of the total post length underground. For a 6-foot fence, use 8-foot posts buried 24-30 inches. In frost-prone areas, go below the frost line (typically 36-42 inches). Gate posts and end posts should be deeper for extra stability.

mainEntity.name: How wide should a post hole be?

acceptedAnswer.text: The hole diameter should be 3 times the post width. For a 4×4 post (actual 3.5"), use an 8-10" hole. For a 6×6 post (actual 5.5"), use a 12" hole. Wider holes provide more concrete mass and better stability.

mainEntity.name: Can I use fast-setting concrete for fence posts?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Fast-setting concrete like Quikrete Fast-Setting can be poured dry into the hole and then wetted — no mixing required. It sets in 20-40 minutes. Regular concrete mix works too but requires mixing and takes 24-48 hours to set.

mainEntity.name: Should I use 60 lb or 80 lb bags for post holes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Either works. 80 lb bags are more cost-effective but heavier to carry. 60 lb bags are easier to handle at remote post locations. Coverage: 60 lb bag = 0.45 cu ft, 80 lb bag = 0.60 cu ft.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate concrete volume for a post hole?

acceptedAnswer.text: Calculate the cylinder volume of the hole (π × radius² × depth) minus the post volume (width × depth × depth for square posts). Post holes are cylinders, so the formula is: π × (hole_radius)² × depth − post_width² × depth, all in consistent units.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Post Hole Concrete

The calculator finds the cylinder volume of the hole, subtracts the volume occupied by the post, and divides by the yield per bag.

Concrete = π × r² × depth − post_area × depth
  • Hole volume: π × (hole_diameter/2)² × depth
  • Post volume: post_width × post_depth × hole_depth
  • Concrete needed: hole volume − post volume
  • Bags: concrete volume ÷ yield per bag (0.60 cu ft for 80 lb)
  • Hole diameter should be 3× the post width
  • Depth should be 1/3 of total post length or below frost line
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many bags of concrete per fence post?

A 4×4 post in a 10" hole at 24" deep needs about 1-2 bags of 80 lb concrete. At 36" deep, plan for 2-3 bags. Larger 6×6 posts displace more concrete so need fewer bags per hole.

How deep should post holes be?

Bury 1/3 of total post length. For a 6 ft fence with 8 ft posts, go 24-30" deep. In frost areas, dig below the frost line (36-42"). Gate and end posts should be deeper.

How wide should post holes be?

3× the post width. For 4×4 posts (3.5" actual), use 8-10" holes. For 6×6 posts (5.5" actual), use 12" holes.

Can I use fast-setting concrete?

Yes. Pour dry into the hole and add water — no mixing needed. Sets in 20-40 minutes. Regular mix works but takes 24-48 hours.

60 lb or 80 lb bags?

80 lb bags are more economical. 60 lb bags are easier to carry to remote post locations. Coverage: 60 lb = 0.45 cu ft, 80 lb = 0.60 cu ft.

How is post hole volume calculated?

Cylinder volume (pi × radius² × depth) minus the rectangular post volume. All converted to cubic feet, then divided by bag yield.
